title: Linux Is on the Move - Up!
author: Bill Claybrook
source_file: 2001 Linux Is on the Move - Up!.pdf
abstract: This Aberdeen InSight argues that Linux development is moving faster than any other commercial operating system and will leapfrog Windows to replace Unix on the high end within seven to eight years. With major contributors including Compaq, HP, IBM, and numerous smaller companies, Aberdeen predicts Linux will join IBM z/OS and Microsoft Windows as a primary server OS before the end of the decade. The study positions Linux as the third pillar of enterprise server computing.
importance_rationale: Published in July 2001 before Linux's enterprise breakthrough was fully established, this study served as an early mainstream analyst endorsement of Linux as an enterprise server OS contender, lending Aberdeen's credibility to the Linux adoption narrative.
relevance_rationale: Linux has more than validated this prediction, making the study primarily of historical interest as a record of early analyst consensus. The specific competitive dynamics (Unix displacement) are now settled but the trajectory analysis methodology remains instructive.
prescience_rationale: The core prediction — Linux joining z/OS and Windows as a primary server OS before decade's end — proved accurate. Linux achieved 28.3% server market share by 2004 and became the dominant server platform by the early 2010s. The seven-to-eight year Unix leapfrog prediction was broadly correct.
